Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables, source URLs, checksums, and function stubs (`prepare()`, `package()`). No code executes at global scope or in `pkgver()` (which is absent). The source array downloads a tarball from the official GitHub releases URL (`https://github.com/moonrepo/moon/releases/download/v...`) with pinned SHA256 checksums. There are no command substitutions, obfuscated strings, or dangerous operations (eval, curl|bash, etc.) that could execute during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are not invoked by these commands, so their content is out of scope for this gate. No supply-chain attack indicators are present.
</details>
<summary>Safe: no malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD downloads a precompiled binary from the official upstream GitHub releases (moonrepo/moon) with pinned SHA-256 checksums. The `prepare()` function runs the downloaded binary only to generate shell completion files, a standard practice for CLIs that produce their own completions. The `package()` function installs the binary, license, and completions using normal `install` commands. There are no unexpected network requests, no obfuscated code, no execution of unchecked content (the source is verified by checksums), and no modifications to system files outside the package directory. The workflow is consistent with a standard AUR binary package and shows no evidence of supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary package with pinned checksums. No malicious indicators.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/moon-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-my_t4mjc...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-my_t4mjc
Analyzing moon-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-my_t4mjc) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source URLs that point directly to GitHub release binaries for the moon tool, specific to the target architecture (x86_64 and aarch64). The `source` array uses verbatim HTTPS URLs to the project's own release assets, which is a normal and expected practice for binary packages. The `sha256sums` are provided and fixed for each architecture, meaning the integrity of the downloaded binaries will be verified—there is no use of 'SKIP' or other insecure placeholders.

There is no top-level code execution, command substitution, or dynamic evaluation outside of standard variable assignments. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version resolution occurs that could introduce remote execution risk. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are defined but will not be executed due to the `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` flags. Therefore, sourcing the PKGBUILD and fetching sources during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` and `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` poses no risk of executing malicious code or exfiltrating data.

The package follows standard AUR packaging practices with no signs of obfuscation, suspicious network calls, or unsafe sourcing. All operations during the current phase are limited to downloading and verifying prebuilt binaries from the official upstream repository.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No malicious code in sourcing or download phase
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: No malicious code in sourcing or download phase[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built binaries from the official GitHub repository of the moon project, specifically from the release page corresponding to version 2.3.4. The sources are correctly specified for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, and appropriate SHA-256 checksums are provided for each, ensuring integrity. The build process consists of running the downloaded binary to generate shell completion files (bash, zsh, fish), which is a legitimate and expected use of the tool during packaging. The binary is then installed to /usr/bin, along with its license and completions, using standard `install` commands.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, unauthorized network requests, or execution of downloaded scripts. All operations occur within the build directory and target the package directory as expected. The prepare() step uses the downloaded binary to self-generate completions — a common practice for modern CLI tools. No `git pull`, `curl | bash`, or other risky operations are present. The maintainer follows secure packaging practices by pinning a specific release version and providing verifiable checksums.

This PKGBUILD adheres to standard AUR packaging conventions and poses no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Official binary release with valid checksums and standard packaging
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration for nvchecker, a version checking tool commonly used in AUR packages to track upstream releases. It specifies checking the latest release tag from the GitHub repository `moonrepo/moon`, with the `v` prefix, which is standard practice for versioned releases.

All fields are straightforward and point to the legitimate upstream project. There are no network requests to suspicious domains, no use of obfuscated commands, and no indication of malicious behavior. The configuration only defines how to retrieve version information and does not execute code or modify system state.

This file is part of normal version-tracking workflow in AUR and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Safe configuration for upstream version tracking
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/4] Reviewing src/moon_cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/README.md, src/moon_cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Safe configuration for upstream version tracking
LLM auditresponse for README.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `README.md` file is a standard documentation file that describes the purpose, features, and benefits of the `moon` tool. It contains no executable code, scripts, or commands that could pose a security risk. The content is purely informational, outlining the project's goals such as improved developer experience, incremental adoption, and integrated toolchain management.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, network requests, file operations, or any other behavior associated with supply-chain attacks. The links present point to official project resources (e.g., documentation, contributing guidelines, code of conduct) and a standard GitHub contributors image badge from contrib.rocks, which is commonly used in open-source projects for visual attribution. These elements are benign and consistent with typical open-source repository practices.

All features described align with the stated purpose of moon as a repository management and build orchestration tool written in Rust. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, unauthorized downloads, or malicious automation. The file does not attempt to execute or instruct the execution of unsafe operations.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation-only file, no executable content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/4] Reviewing src/moon_cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/CHANGELOG.md...
+ Reviewed src/moon_cli-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/README.md. Status: SAFE -- Documentation-only file, no executable content
LLM auditresponse for CHANGELOG.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a CHANGELOG.md, which documents version updates, features, fixes, and internal changes for the moon-bin package. It contains no executable code, scripts, or configuration that could introduce security risks such as remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unauthorized system modifications.

The content reflects standard development practices, including updates to toolchain integrations (e.g., Python, Rust, Go), performance improvements, bug fixes, and configuration enhancements. Mentions of downloading plugins via `ghcr.io` refer to legitimate container registry usage for reliable and secure distribution, not malicious payloads. References to daemon functionality, caching, and task execution pertain to core application functionality, not supply-chain attacks.

There is no evidence of obfuscated code, suspicious network activity to unknown hosts, or use of dangerous commands (e.g., curl|bash). All changes are consistent with the expected evolution of a build orchestration tool. The file does not execute anything and serves only documentation purposes.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation-only file, no executable code
</summary>
</security_assessment>
